request.question: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?

title: Rock 'n' Roll Singer
I Hate Rock 'n' Roll
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll
Drive My Car
Rock 'n' Roll (John Lennon album)
Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)
World Rock'n'Roll Confederation
What's Next to the Moon (album)
It's Only Rock 'n Roll
Iowa Rock 'n' Roll Hall of Fame
sentences: Rock 'n' Roll Singer is the debut solo EP from Mark Kozelek.

The EP was released on June 13, 2000, and was released while Kozelek's final album with his previous band Red House Painters (the 1998 album "Old Ramon", which didn't get a release until 2001) was in limbo with record label mergers.

"Rock 'n' Roll Singer" contains three original tracks and four covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie" originally by AC/DC, and "Around and Around" originally by John Denver.
"I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.
It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".
It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.
This release was the last release for the band on this label.
The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.
The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.
Just... Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll is a studio album by Cliff Richard, released 11 November 2016.
The album continues the rock 'n' roll theme of his previous studio album "The Fabulous Rock 'n' Roll Songbook".
It comprises covers of 14 classic rock 'n' roll songs and one new song "It's Better to Dream".
It features Elvis Presley in duet with Richard in "Blue Suede Shoes" and Peter Frampton on guitar in "Dimples".
The album reached number 4 on the UK Albums Chart and has been certified Gold for sales over 100,000 in the UK.
"Drive My Car" is a song by the Beatles, written primarily by Paul McCartney, with lyrical contributions from John Lennon.
It was first released on the British version of the band's 1965 album "Rubber Soul"; it also appeared in North America on the "Yesterday and Today" collection.
The upbeat, lighthearted "Drive My Car" was used as the opening track for both albums.
Over the years the song has been covered by many artists such as former Spice Girls member Melanie C and Lulu, and the US band Breakfast Club.
which was featured in the 1988 film "License to Drive", that was played over the opening credits.
Rock 'n' Roll is the sixth studio album by John Lennon.
Released in 1975, it is an album of late 1950s and early 1960s songs as covered by Lennon.

Recording the album was problematic and spanned an entire year: Phil Spector produced sessions in October 1973 at A&M Studios, and Lennon produced sessions in October 1974 at Record Plant Studios (East).
Lennon was being sued by Morris Levy over copyright infringement of one line in his song "Come Together".
As part of an agreement, Lennon had to include three Levy-owned songs on "Rock 'n' Roll".
Spector disappeared with the session recordings and was subsequently involved in a motor accident, leaving the album's tracks unrecoverable until the beginning of the "Walls and Bridges" sessions.
With "Walls and Bridges" coming out first, featuring one Levy-owned song, Levy sued Lennon expecting to see Lennon's "Rock 'n' Roll" album.
Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.
The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.

The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.
"Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help!
" single.
World Rock 'n' Roll Confederation (WRRC) was registered in 1984, although its history traces to 1974.
It is an umbrella organization for national professional and amateur Rock and Roll dancesport federations.

Its statute that it "aims at promoting the physical training of its members by means of sporting activities in the form of Rock 'n' Roll dance tournaments, including the acrobatic variations (acrobatic rock 'n' roll) as well as Rock 'n' Roll and Boogie Woogie, Lindy Hop, Formation and alternative styles in line with the rules and sporting presentations".
The registered office is in Ljubljana, Slovenia.
What's Next to the Moon is a solo album by Mark Kozelek, released on January 10, 2001.
The album is composed entirely of drastically rearranged acoustic covers of Bon Scott-era AC/DC songs.
The album follows the release of his debut solo EP "Rock 'n' Roll Singer", which also features three AC/DC covers: "Rock 'n' Roll Singer," "You Ain't Got a Hold on Me," and "Bad Boy Boogie."
The versions of "Rock 'n' Roll Singer" and "Bad Boy Boogie" on this album are different from the versions on the EP.
It's Only Rock 'n Roll is the 12th British and 14th American studio album by The Rolling Stones, released in 1974.
It was the last Rolling Stones album for guitarist Mick Taylor and the songwriting and recording of the album's title track had a connection to Taylor's eventual replacement, Ronnie Wood.
The album also marked the 10th anniversary since the release of the band's debut album, "The Rolling Stones".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" has a firmer rock sound than the band's previous album, the more funk - and soul - inspired "Goats Head Soup".
"It's Only Rock 'n Roll" reached #1 in the US and #2 in the UK.
The Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Hall of Fame is a museum located in Arnolds Park, Iowa and maintained by the non-profit Iowa Rock ‘n’ Roll Music Association (IRRMA).
The mission of IRRMA is to “retain and honor the legacy of rock ’n’ roll music and preserve the history of music in the state of Iowa.”

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Rock 'n' Roll Music was an American compilation of album tracks, including the 1965 album opener, with cover versions released by which iconic band?
hop1_docs: The Pretty Things (album) | The Pretty Things is the self-titled 1965 release by the English rock band The Pretty Things, which features mostly R&B and rock and roll cover versions.  A re-issue released in 2000 on CD featured every track from both the American and British versions of the album.

I Hate Rock 'n' Roll | "I Hate Rock 'n' Roll" is a song by the Scottish alternative rock group The Jesus and Mary Chain.  It was the first single from the band's compilation album, "Hate Rock 'n' Roll".  It was released together with 3 other tracks by Blanco y Negro Records in June 1995 and reached #61 in the UK single charts.  This release was the last release for the band on this label.  The 10" format was numbered and limited to 5000 copies.  The reworked version of the track, which was dubbed as "I Love Rock 'n' Roll" was also included on the group's last album "Munki", released in 1998.

Rock 'n' Roll Music (album) | Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.  The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.  The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.  "Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help! " single.

Banquet (album) | Banquet is the fourth album by German progressive rock band Lucifer's Friend, released in 1974.  By this time there was no hard rock sound, it was now strictly progressive rock mixed with jazz fusion.  The song "Our World Is a Rock 'n' Roll Band" was the opening track on some versions (i.e. the U.S. version) of the original LP release, but the only CD releases to date do not include this track, which can instead be found as a bonus track on the Repertoire Records CD release of their self-titled debut.

Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets | Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets is a compilation album by American rock singer–songwriter Bob Seger.  The double-disc album was released on November 21, 2011 and contains 26 remastered tracks from throughout Seger's career, which spans more than four decades.  Included are the original mono version of "Ramblin' Gamblin' Man", Seger's first hit with The Bob Seger System from 1968, the classic Christmas song "The Little Drummer Boy" from 1987's "A Very Special Christmas", which makes its first appearance on a Seger album, and previously unreleased cover versions of Tom Waits' "Downtown Train" and Little Richard's "Hey, Hey, Hey, Hey (Going Back to Birmingham)."  There is also a Walmart exclusive edition that includes the bonus track "Living Inside My Heart," a song from the soundtrack of the 1986 film "About Last Night...", which has also never before been released on any Bob Seger album.  Two songs on this compilation album are edited compared to the original releases: "We've Got Tonight" is the single edit, which is about one minute shorter than the album version, and "Katmandu" is a newly edited version which omits the second verse, making the song also about one minute shorter compared to the original album version.  In the US it was certified gold and platinum in June 2013 by the RIAA.

His Best (Bo Diddley album) | His Best is a 1997 greatest hits compilation album by American rock and roll icon Bo Diddley released by Chess and MCA Records on April 8, 1997 (see 1997 in music).  The album was re-released by Geffen Records on April 17, 2007 as The Definitive Collection with a different album cover.  "The Definitive Collection" reached #2 on "Billboard" magazine's Blues Albums chart on June 21, 2008, which was the week that the album debuted on the charts.

Icon (Blink-182 album) | Icon is a compilation album from the American rock band Blink-182, released on March 29, 2013.  "Icon" was released following the band's departure from Interscope Records in 2012 and compiles the band's biggest singles, covering material from "Dude Ranch" (1997) to "Blink-182" (2003).
hop1_titles: Ultimate Hits: Rock and Roll Never Forgets
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles" — [3]
- '"Drive My Car",' — [3]
- '"I'm Down"' — [3]
- '"a dozen cover versions"' — [3]
- "Chuck Berry" — [3]
- "Little Richard" — [3]
- "Carl Perkins" — [3]
- "Larry Williams" — [3]
- "7 June 1976" — [3]

Ambiguities:
- "1965 album opener" is not explicitly labeled in the passages; the passages list "Drive My Car" but do not state "1965 album opener" verbatim.
- No other band candidate for the compilation artist appears in the provided passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages explicitly identify the compilation's releasing artist as "The Beatles" (so the core question is answerable).
- The only minor missing linkage is an explicit statement in these passages that the "1965 album opener" phrasing in the question refers to "Drive My Car"; that mapping is not spelled out here.

Retrieval direction:
- Open "Rock 'n' Roll Music (album)" (Wikipedia) to confirm tracklist and compilation credits.
- Open "Drive My Car" (Beatles song) (Wikipedia) to confirm it was the 1965 album opener (Rubber Soul).
- Open "Rubber Soul" (Wikipedia) to verify the album opener and date.
- (Optional) Search query: "Drive My Car cover versions" for recordings/releases by other artists.

Conclusion: The passages identify the compilation as by The Beatles; final answer: The Beatles. [[ ## completed ## ]]
response.hop2_query: Rock 'n' Roll Music artist The Beatles
hop2_docs: Rock 'n' Roll Music (album) | Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles that consists of previously released Beatles tracks.  The double album was issued on 7 June 1976 in the United States, on Capitol Records (catalogue number SKBO 11537), and on Parlophone (PCSP 719) in the United Kingdom, four days later.  The album is a combination of some notable Lennon–McCartney originals, such as "Drive My Car", "Revolution", "Back in the U.S.S.R." and "Get Back", George Harrison's "Taxman", and a dozen cover versions of songs written by significant rock and roll composers of the 1950s, including Chuck Berry, Little Richard, Carl Perkins and Larry Williams.  "Rock 'n' Roll Music" was the first Beatles album to include "I'm Down", which had previously only been available as the B-side of the "Help! " single.

(I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz | (I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz is a 1995 tribute album by various jazz artists and bands from the GRP Records label.  It consists of jazz cover versions of songs originally by The Beatles.  The album's title comes from the lyrics of the Beatles's cover of the Chuck Berry song, "Rock and Roll Music", which was originally released on the studio album "Beatles for Sale".

Classic Rock (Westwood One) | Classic Rock (formerly The Classic Rock Experience prior to 2011) was a 24-hour music format produced by Cumulus Media Networks (now Westwood One).  It drew an adult mainstream audience between the ages of 25-49 with classic rock music from artists such as Aerosmith, The Allman Brothers Band, The Beatles, Phil Collins, Deep Purple, The Eagles, Jimi Hendrix, Pink Floyd, Queen, and The Rolling Stones.

Sekret (band) | Sekret (Russian: Секрет , lit.: "Secret") was a Soviet Russian rock and roll band founded in 1982 in Leningrad.  The band's musical style and image drawn a heavy inspiration from 1950's and 1960's western music, The Beatles in particular.  The band's name was inspired by the Beatles song 'Do You Want to Know a Secret? '.  Secret called themselves a "Beat Quartet" and were a part of Soviet rock and roll revival of 1980's, along with Bravo.

The Rock'n'Roll Era (Time-Life Music) | The Rock'n'Roll Era was a 53-volume series issued by Time-Life during the late 1980s and early 1990s, spotlighting songs from the early years of the rock and roll era, primarily the pre-Beatles era of 1954-1964.  The first major and successful rock music-intensive subscription music series released by Time-Life, songs on the series included music heard on mainstream contemporary hit radio stations of the late 1950s and early to mid-1960s.

Roll Over Beethoven | "Roll Over Beethoven" is a 1956 hit single written by Chuck Berry, originally released on Chess Records, with "Drifting Heart" as the B-side.  The lyrics of the song mention rock and roll and the desire for rhythm and blues to replace classical music.  The title of the song is an imperative directed at the composer Ludwig van Beethoven to roll over in his grave in reaction to the new genre of music that Berry was promoting.  The song has been covered by many other artists, including the Beatles and the Electric Light Orchestra. " Rolling Stone" magazine ranked it number 97 on its list of the "500 Greatest Songs of All Time".

All I've Got to Do | "All I've Got to Do" is a song written by John Lennon (credited to Lennon–McCartney) and performed by English rock group the Beatles on their second British album, "With the Beatles".  In the United States, "All I've Got to Do" originally appeared on "Meet the Beatles! ".  According to Dennis Alstrand, this song is the first time in rock and roll or rock music where the bass player plays chords as a vital part of the song.
hop2_titles: (I Got No Kick Against) Modern Jazz
response.summary_2: Rock 'n' Roll Music is a compilation album by The Beatles.
